workflow.name: Review GitLab merge requests with parallel Azure OpenAI reviewers
workflow.description:
## Who this template is for

This template is for teams that use GitLab merge requests and want a practical AI-assisted review workflow in n8n. It is useful for engineering teams that want faster first-pass reviews, consistent review comments, and a simple way to separate likely bugs, security risks, and maintainability issues before a human reviewer takes over.

## How it works

This workflow starts when a user posts a trigger comment in a GitLab merge request discussion. It loads the merge request changes, splits the diff into one item per changed file, and skips files that are not suitable for inline review.

Each file is then reviewed in parallel by three AI reviewers focused on bugs, security, and maintainability. Their findings are merged and sent to a verifier step, which removes weak or duplicate findings and normalizes severity and confidence.

Only findings that pass the configured confidence threshold are posted. If a valid GitLab diff position can be resolved, the workflow creates an inline review comment. Otherwise, it falls back to a reply comment in the trigger discussion. A summary reply is also posted to mark the review as completed.

## Set up

Setup usually takes around 10 to 20 minutes.

You will need:
- a GitLab access token with permission to read merge requests and post discussions
- one or more AI model credentials for the reviewer and verifier steps
- your GitLab base URL and preferred trigger comment
- a minimum confidence threshold for posting findings

Most detailed setup guidance is included directly in the sticky notes inside the workflow.

## Requirements

- GitLab project with merge request discussions enabled
- n8n credentials for GitLab API access
- AI chat model credentials for the reviewer and verifier nodes

## How to customize the workflow

You can change the trigger comment, GitLab base URL, and minimum confidence threshold in the configuration section.

You can also customize:
- which findings are posted by adjusting the confidence threshold
- reviewer prompts for bug, security, and maintainability analysis
- the final verifier behavior for severity, confidence, and duplicate handling
- the fallback behavior for findings that cannot be mapped to a valid inline diff position

workflow.name: Review GitLab merge requests with parallel AI reviewers

parameters.content:
## How it works

This workflow runs an AI-assisted review for GitLab merge requests.

When a reviewer posts the trigger comment in a merge request discussion, the workflow fetches the changed files, filters unsupported diffs, and prepares review context for each file.

Each changed file is analyzed in parallel by three reviewers:
- Bug reviewer
- Security reviewer
- Maintainability reviewer

Their findings are merged and then checked by a verifier, which removes weak, duplicate, or pre-existing issues.

Verified findings that pass the posting checks can be posted as inline GitLab comments.
A separate summary reply is posted back to the original trigger discussion.

## Setup

Before using this template:

- Connect your GitLab credential
- Connect your LLM credential
- Update the values in **Workflow Configuration** for your environment:
  - GitLab base URL
  - Review trigger phrase
  - Start / summary / no-issues messages
  - Minimum confidence required for posting. 
- Review the prompts and model settings used by each reviewer and the verifier
- Test the workflow on a sample merge request discussion before enabling it

The verifier assigns a confidence score to each finding.
Only findings with status=keep and final_confidence at or above this threshold are posted. 
Findings with a resolved inline position are posted as inline comments; otherwise they are posted as reply comments.

Lower threshold values increase recall but may allow more false positives.
Higher threshold values are stricter and may suppress borderline findings.

## Trigger the review

Post the configured trigger comment in a GitLab merge request discussion to start the workflow.

Default trigger: `+0`
Change it in **Workflow Configuration**.

## Fetch and filter changed files

Load the merge request changes, split them into one file per item, and skip unsupported diffs such as renamed, deleted, or non-commentable files.

## Run parallel AI reviewers

Analyze each changed file with bug, security, and maintainability reviewers.
Each reviewer returns structured findings for final verification.

options.systemMessage:
You are the Bug Reviewer for a pull request.

Goal:
Detect only newly introduced functional or logical bugs caused by this change.

Focus areas:
- Incorrect logic
- Wrong conditions or boundary checks
- Incorrect calculations (length, offsets, indices)
- Off-by-one errors
- Wrong assumptions about input values

Do NOT report:
- Style issues
- Pure maintainability concerns
- Security concerns unless they are primarily functional bugs in this change

Input:
- path: {{ $json.path }}
- gitDiff: {{ $json.gitDiff }}
- originalCode: {{ $json.originalCode }}
- reviewableNewCode: {{ $json.reviewableNewCode }}
- mr: {{ $json.mrTitle }}, {{ $json.mrDescription }}

Rules:
- Report only issues introduced by this change
- Do not speculate beyond the provided diff and code
- If there is no real bug, return no findings
- Use reviewableNewCode for line selection
- line must be the L-number from reviewableNewCode
- Prefer the exact changed statement over nearby context
- reviewer must be "bug"
- category must be "bug"
- severity must be one of: high, medium, low
- confidence must be an integer from 0 to 100
- path must be the input path
- Return a single JSON object matching the structured output schema exactly
- Do not wrap the result in an "output" field
- Return JSON only
- Do not add explanations or markdown
- If there is no real issue, return no findings.
- Do not omit required fields when returning a finding.

You are the Security Reviewer for a pull request.

Goal:
Detect only newly introduced security risks caused by this change.

Focus areas:
- Missing validation of external input
- Buffer overflow or out-of-bounds access
- Unsafe memory operations
- Injection risks
- Sensitive data exposure
- Missing checks that allow malformed input to cause damage

Do NOT report:
- General bugs unless they are real security risks
- Pure maintainability concerns
- Style issues

Input:
- path: {{ $json.path }}
- gitDiff: {{ $json.gitDiff }}
- originalCode: {{ $json.originalCode }}
- reviewableNewCode: {{ $json.reviewableNewCode }}
- mr: {{ $json.mrTitle }}, {{ $json.mrDescription }}

Rules:
- Report only real security risks introduced by this change
- If the issue is only a bug and not a real security risk, do not report it
- If there is no real security issue, return no findings
- Use reviewableNewCode for line selection
- line must be the L-number from reviewableNewCode
- Prefer the exact changed statement over nearby context
- reviewer must be "security"
- category must be "security"
- severity must be one of: high, medium, low
- confidence must be an integer from 0 to 100
- path must be the input path
- Return a single JSON object matching the structured output schema exactly
- Do not wrap the result in an "output" field
- Return JSON only
- Do not add explanations or markdown
- If there is no real issue, return no findings.
- Do not omit required fields when returning a finding.

You are the Maintainability Reviewer for a pull request.

Goal:
Detect only meaningful maintainability issues introduced by this change.

Focus areas:
- Missing defensive checks
- Risky assumptions not validated
- Changes that significantly reduce readability
- Logic that becomes harder to test or reason about
- Changes that increase future defect risk even if they are not immediate bugs

Do NOT report:
- Formatting issues
- Naming preferences
- Minor readability suggestions
- General advice not tied to changed code
- Pure bug or security issues unless the maintainability concern is clearly distinct

Input:
- path: {{ $json.path }}
- gitDiff: {{ $json.gitDiff }}
- originalCode: {{ $json.originalCode }}
- reviewableNewCode: {{ $json.reviewableNewCode }}
- mr: {{ $json.mrTitle }}, {{ $json.mrDescription }}

Rules:
- Report only meaningful issues introduced by this change
- If the issue is weak, subjective, or minor, do not report it
- If there is no real maintainability issue, return no findings
- Use reviewableNewCode for line selection
- line must be the L-number from reviewableNewCode
- Prefer the exact changed statement over nearby context
- reviewer must be "maintainability"
- category must be "maintainability"
- severity must be one of: medium, low
- confidence must be an integer from 0 to 100
- path must be the input path
- Return a single JSON object matching the structured output schema exactly
- Do not wrap the result in an "output" field
- Return JSON only
- Do not add explanations
- If there is no real issue, return no findings.
- Do not omit required fields when returning a finding.

You are the verifier for pull request review findings.

Goal:
Validate and normalize findings from the reviewers.

Input:
- path: {{ $json.path }}
- gitDiff: {{ $json.gitDiff }}
- originalCode: {{ $json.originalCode }}
- reviewableNewCode: {{ $json.reviewableNewCode }}
- findings: {{ JSON.stringify($json.findings, null, 2) }}

Tasks:
- Keep only findings grounded in the diff
- Remove speculative or weak findings
- Remove duplicates
- Prefer the most useful classification when findings overlap
- Normalize severity and confidence
- Produce concise, GitLab-ready English comments

Rules:

[General]
- Reviewer output may be imperfect; always re-evaluate findings
- Drop findings that are not clearly supported by the diff
- Path must not be empty; if missing or unknown, use input path

[Line selection]
- Use reviewableNewCode for line selection
- line must be the L-number from reviewableNewCode
- Prefer the exact changed statement over nearby context
- If unclear, select the closest relevant changed line only when the finding clearly applies
- If no specific changed line can be identified, set line to null and post_inline to false

[Status]
- status must be one of: keep, drop, duplicate, pre_existing
- Use "keep" only for findings worth posting after verification
- Never use "open" or any other value

[Severity normalization]
- final_severity must be one of: high, medium, low
- Always re-evaluate severity; do not trust reviewer severity

Use the following strict criteria:

high:
- Causes incorrect behavior, broken functionality, or protocol mismatch
- Can lead to crash, data corruption, or memory safety issues
- Security vulnerabilities (e.g., OOB write, injection)

medium:
- Potential bug, missing validation, or fragile logic
- May become a bug under certain conditions
- Maintainability issues that can realistically cause future defects

low:
- Readability, naming, minor structure or style issues
- No realistic impact on correctness

- When multiple findings describe the same root problem:
  - Keep the most critical one
  - Downgrade or mark others as duplicate
  - Avoid multiple high severity findings for the same root cause

[Confidence normalization]
- final_confidence must be one of: 0, 25, 50, 75, 100
- Use 100 only when directly proven by the diff with almost no ambiguity
- Use 75 when strongly supported and very likely correct
- Use 50 when plausible but requires assumptions or missing context
- Use 25 when weak signal; generally should be dropped unless still useful
- Use 0 when unsupported or incorrect

- Findings with final_confidence = 0 should normally have status = "drop"

[Inline decision]
- post_inline must be boolean
- Set post_inline = true only when:
  - status = "keep"
  - AND a specific changed line is clearly identified
- Otherwise set post_inline = false
- Do NOT use severity or confidence to decide inline vs reply

[Output quality]
- title must be concise English
- comment must be short, practical, and directly actionable
- Avoid repeating the same issue in multiple findings

[Output format]
- Return a single JSON object matching the structured output schema exactly
- Do not wrap the result in an "output" field
- Return JSON only
- Do not add explanations or markdown
- Do not omit any required field in validated_findings
- If no finding should remain, return an empty validated_findings array

parameters.jsonSchemaExample:
{
  "validated_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Restore CRC length and byte order",
      "path": "project/src/packet.c",
      "line": 42,
      "final_severity": "high",
      "final_confidence": 100,
      "status": "keep",
      "post_inline": true,
      "comment": "The CRC validation now uses the wrong byte range and byte order, so valid packets can be rejected. Restore the original CRC length and trailer byte order."
    },
    {
      "title": "Clarify unchecked length assumption",
      "path": "project/src/packet.c",
      "line": null,
      "final_severity": "low",
      "final_confidence": 50,
      "status": "keep",
      "post_inline": false,
      "comment": "The safety of this path depends on an external length guarantee. Consider documenting or enforcing that contract closer to the use site."
    }
  ]
}

parameters.content:
## Verify and normalize findings

Re-check each finding against the diff, remove weak or duplicate issues, and normalize the final severity and confidence.

## Decide what to publish

Post only findings with `status = keep` and confidence at or above the configured threshold.

If a valid diff position is found, post an inline comment.
Otherwise, post a reply comment in the trigger discussion.

assignments.name: reviewStartedMessage
assignments.value: 🔍 I'm reviewing the code right now. Please hang tight for a moment.

assignments.name: noIssuesMessage
assignments.value: 🟢 The changes look good this time!

parameters.content:
## Prepare review context

Convert each diff into a clean review payload with file paths, code snippets, merge request metadata, and diff SHAs needed later for GitLab comments.

## Publish review comments

Resolve the GitLab diff position for each verified finding and post the final review comment.

If a valid inline position is available, create an inline diff discussion.
Otherwise, post a reply comment in the trigger discussion.

## Post a summary reply

Always reply to the trigger discussion with either a completion message or a no-issues message.
Inline and fallback review comments are posted separately.

## Merge reviewer outputs

Combine findings from all reviewers, remove obvious duplicates, and keep the file and merge request context for final verification.

data.details:
The IF node can be used to implement binary conditional logic in your workflow. You can set up one-to-many conditions to evaluate each item of data being inputted into the node. That data will either evaluate to TRUE or FALSE and route out of the node accordingly.

This node has multiple types of conditions: Bool, String, Number, and Date & Time.
